reasoning: The aspirational, self-imposed deadline for Republicans to deliver the budget reconciliation bill to President Trump's desk is the July 4 holiday, which is already after the July 1 resolution date. Independent policy experts predict passage is more likely to slip to August. The Senate intends to make significant changes to the House-passed bill, including revisions to Medicaid cuts and adjusting the debt ceiling instructions. The Byrd Rule ensures further modifications due to Senate parliamentarian scrubs. These changes will force the bill back to the House for a second vote. Given that the House is out for a week around Juneteenth, the legislative window before July 1 is severely compressed. Navigating a razor-thin 215-214 House margin on an amended mega-bill in less than a week natively makes final passage prior to July 1 extremely unlikely. The May 29 crowd forecast of 4% was accurate, and the probability has only decreased as days have passed without a scheduled Senate floor vote.
